items.title: Big-O Notation: Measuring Algorithm Efficiency
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Big-O Notation

Big-O describes how an algorithm's cost grows as input size `n` grows. It ignores constants and lower-order terms — `3n + 100` is `O(n)`.

## Common classes (fastest → slowest)

| Big-O | Name | Example |
|---|---|---|
| O(1) | Constant | Array index, Dictionary lookup |
| O(log n) | Logarithmic | Binary search |
| O(n) | Linear | Single loop over a list |
| O(n log n) | Linearithmic | Efficient sorting (QuickSort, MergeSort) |
| O(n²) | Quadratic | Nested loops over same input |
| O(2ⁿ) | Exponential | Naive recursive Fibonacci |

## Rules of thumb

- Sequential statements: take the **max** — `O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²)`.
- Nested loops: **multiply** — loop inside a loop over `n` = `O(n²)`.
- Always reason about the **worst case** unless stated otherwise.
- Also track **space complexity**: extra memory allocated, not the input itself.

```csharp
// O(n²): compare every pair
for (int i = 0; i < arr.Length; i++)
    for (int j = i + 1; j < arr.Length; j++)
        if (arr[i] == arr[j]) return true;

// O(n): trade memory for speed
var seen = new HashSet<int>();
foreach (var x in arr)
    if (!seen.Add(x)) return true;
```

That rewrite — nested loop → hash set — is the single most common optimization in interviews and real code.
items.practiceTask: Take a method from your current project with a loop inside a loop (e.g., matching two lists). Determine its Big-O, then rewrite it using a Dictionary or HashSet and measure both with Stopwatch on 10,000 items.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
quiz.explanation: Array elements are stored contiguously; the address is computed directly from the index.

quiz.question: A method runs one O(n) loop, then a separate O(n²) nested loop. Its overall complexity is:
quiz.explanation: Sequential blocks take the dominant term: O(n) + O(n²) simplifies to O(n²).

items.title: Arrays: Memory Layout and Why Cache Matters
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Arrays

An array is a contiguous block of memory. `arr[i]` compiles to `baseAddress + i * elementSize` — that's why indexing is O(1).

## Costs

| Operation | Cost |
|---|---|
| Access by index | O(1) |
| Search unsorted | O(n) |
| Insert/delete at end | O(1) amortized (List<T>) |
| Insert/delete at middle | O(n) — must shift elements |

## Contiguity = cache friendliness

CPUs load memory in 64-byte cache lines. Iterating an `int[]` pulls 16 ints per memory fetch; iterating a linked list jumps to random addresses and misses cache constantly. In practice arrays outperform linked lists even for workloads where big-O says otherwise.

## List<T> internals

`List<T>` wraps an array. When full, it allocates a **new array of double the capacity** and copies. That's why `Add` is *amortized* O(1): occasional O(n) copies averaged over many adds.

```csharp
var list = new List<int>(capacity: 100_000); // pre-size when count is known
```

Pre-sizing avoids repeated grow-and-copy cycles — a cheap, real optimization in hot paths.
items.practiceTask: Benchmark adding 1M items to a List<int> with and without initial capacity using Stopwatch. Then explain the difference in terms of array doubling.

quiz.question: Why is inserting into the middle of an array O(n)?
options: All later elements must shift one position
The whole array is always reallocated
quiz.explanation: Contiguous storage means every element after the insertion point must move to make room.

items.title: Strings, Immutability and StringBuilder
items.practiceTask: Write an IsAnagram(string a, string b) method two ways: sorting both strings (O(n log n)) and counting characters with an int[26] (O(n)). Verify both work, then explain which allocates less.

quiz.question: Building a string with += inside a loop of n iterations costs:
options: O(n²) — each += copies the whole string so far
quiz.explanation: Immutability forces a full copy per concatenation: 1+2+...+n character copies = O(n²).

items.title: Hash Tables: How Dictionary<TKey,TValue> Works
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Hash Tables

`Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` gives O(1) average lookup by turning a key into a bucket index:

1. `key.GetHashCode()` → integer hash.
2. `hash % bucketCount` → bucket index.
3. Bucket holds entries; equal hashes are resolved by chaining through an entries array.

## Collisions

Two keys can hash to the same bucket. .NET chains colliding entries; lookup walks the chain comparing with `Equals`. If *many* keys collide (bad `GetHashCode`), lookup degrades toward O(n).

## The GetHashCode/Equals contract

If you use a custom class as a key you **must** override both, consistently: equal objects must return equal hash codes. Records do this for you:

```csharp
public record CustomerKey(int RegionId, string Code); // value equality + hash for free

var cache = new Dictionary<CustomerKey, decimal>();
```

Mutating a key object after inserting it breaks retrieval — the stored hash no longer matches. Prefer immutable keys.

## Cost table

| Operation | Average | Worst |
|---|---|---|
| Add / Lookup / Remove | O(1) | O(n) (mass collisions) |

Rule: whenever you write `list.FirstOrDefault(x => x.Id == id)` inside a loop, you probably want a Dictionary built once outside the loop.
items.practiceTask: Find a spot in your codebase where a List is searched inside a loop (FirstOrDefault/Any per iteration). Replace it with a Dictionary built via ToDictionary() and compare complexity before/after.

quiz.question: Two different keys land in the same bucket. This is called:
quiz.explanation: Collisions are normal; the dictionary chains entries and compares keys with Equals to resolve them.

quiz.question: You use a mutable class as a Dictionary key, insert it, then change a field used in GetHashCode. What happens?
options: The entry may become unreachable via lookup
quiz.explanation: The entry was placed by the old hash. Lookups now compute a different hash and search the wrong bucket.

items.title: Linked Lists: Trade-offs vs Arrays
items.practiceTask: Implement a singly linked list node class and write two methods: Reverse() and HasCycle() using fast/slow pointers. Test with a list you deliberately make cyclic.

quiz.question: In Floyd's cycle detection, why do fast (2 steps) and slow (1 step) pointers meet if a cycle exists?
options: They both restart at head periodically
Inside a cycle the gap shrinks by 1 each step, so it reaches 0
quiz.explanation: Once both are in the cycle, fast gains one position per step on slow, so the distance decreases to zero.

items.title: Stacks: LIFO and Its Surprising Uses
items.practiceTask: Implement IsBalanced above and extend it to report the position of the first mismatched bracket. Then rewrite a simple recursive function (e.g., directory traversal) using an explicit Stack<T> instead of recursion.

quiz.question: Which problem is a natural fit for a stack?
options: Finding the minimum in a rotating window
quiz.explanation: Nesting is inherently LIFO: the most recently opened bracket must close first.

items.title: Queues: FIFO, Deques, and PriorityQueue
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Queues

First-In-First-Out. `Queue<T>`: `Enqueue`, `Dequeue`, `Peek` — all O(1) (circular buffer internally).

## The family

| Type | Behavior | .NET |
|---|---|---|
| Queue | FIFO | `Queue<T>` |
| Deque | push/pop both ends | `LinkedList<T>` (or two-stack trick) |
| Priority queue | dequeue smallest priority | `PriorityQueue<TElement,TPriority>` (.NET 6+) |

## Where you already use them

- **BFS traversal** — the queue *is* the algorithm (level by level).
- **Message queues** — RabbitMQ/Azure Service Bus are distributed FIFO queues; same mental model.
- **Channel<T>** — producer/consumer pipelines in ASP.NET Core background services are queues with async semantics.

```csharp
var pq = new PriorityQueue<string, int>();
pq.Enqueue("low", 5);
pq.Enqueue("urgent", 1);
pq.Dequeue(); // "urgent" — smallest priority first
```

`PriorityQueue` is a binary heap: Enqueue/Dequeue are O(log n) — it powers Dijkstra's algorithm and any "process cheapest first" scheduling.
items.practiceTask: Build a mini job scheduler: enqueue jobs with priorities into PriorityQueue<Job,int> and process them in order. Then simulate a print queue with Queue<T> and compare the dequeue order.

quiz.question: PriorityQueue<TElement,TPriority> in .NET dequeues:
options: The element with the smallest priority value
quiz.explanation: It's a min-heap: the smallest priority is always at the root and dequeued first.

items.title: Two Pointers: O(n²) → O(n)
items.practiceTask: Solve: given a sorted int[], square every element and return the result sorted, in O(n), using two pointers from both ends (largest absolute values are at the edges).

quiz.question: Why does the opposite-ends two-pointer approach require sorted input?
options: Pointers can't move in unsorted arrays
Sortedness makes 'move left up = bigger sum' a valid decision rule
It doesn't — it works on any array
quiz.explanation: The algorithm relies on knowing which direction changes the sum monotonically; that's only true when sorted.

items.title: Sliding Window: Substrings and Subarrays in O(n)
items.practiceTask: Solve: minimum length of a contiguous subarray whose sum ≥ target (variable window: expand right, shrink left while sum stays ≥ target). Test edge cases: no valid window, whole array needed.

quiz.question: Why is the variable sliding window O(n) even though it has a nested-looking while loop?
options: The while loop runs at most log n times
left and right each advance at most n times total across the whole run
Dictionary operations make it O(1) overall
quiz.explanation: Amortized analysis: each pointer only moves forward, so total work across all iterations is 2n.

items.title: Binary Search: Beyond Finding an Element
items.practiceTask: Implement FirstTrue(int lo, int hi, Func<int,bool> condition) returning the smallest value where condition flips to true. Use it to find the first version where a fake IsBuildBroken(v) returns true.

quiz.question: Why write mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2?
options: It avoids integer overflow when lo + hi exceeds int.MaxValue
It rounds up instead of down
quiz.explanation: lo + hi can overflow for large indices; lo + (hi-lo)/2 mathematically equals it without the overflow risk.

items.title: Recursion: Base Case, Progress, Trust
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Recursion

A function that calls itself on a smaller subproblem. Every correct recursion has:

1. **Base case** — smallest input, answered directly.
2. **Progress** — each call moves toward the base case.
3. **Trust** — assume the recursive call is correct and combine its result (the leap of faith).

```csharp
long SizeOf(DirectoryInfo dir) =>
    dir.GetFiles().Sum(f => f.Length) +
    dir.GetDirectories().Sum(SizeOf);   // trust: each subdir returns its size
```

## The call stack is finite

~1MB default stack in .NET ≈ tens of thousands of frames. Deep recursion → `StackOverflowException` (uncatchable!). Linear recursion over big data should become a loop or explicit `Stack<T>`.

## Cost analysis

- One recursive call per level (factorial, directory sum): O(depth).
- **Two** calls per level (naive Fibonacci): O(2ⁿ) — the tree of calls doubles each level. Memoization fixes this (later lesson).

Recursion shines on **recursive structures**: trees, JSON, org charts, file systems, expression parsing. If the data nests, recursion reads naturally; if it's flat, prefer a loop.
items.practiceTask: Write a recursive method that pretty-prints a nested menu structure (e.g., categories with children) with indentation. Then rewrite it with an explicit Stack<(node, depth)> and confirm identical output.

quiz.question: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) is exponential because:
options: The same subproblems are recomputed exponentially many times
quiz.explanation: fib(40) computes fib(38) twice, fib(37) three times, etc. — the call tree has ~2ⁿ nodes.

items.title: Sorting I: What O(n log n) Buys You
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Sorting Fundamentals

Comparison sorting can't beat **O(n log n)** in the general case — that's a proven lower bound. The O(n²) sorts still matter for understanding:

| Sort | Time | Space | Stable | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble | O(n²) | O(1) | Yes | Educational only |
| Insertion | O(n²), O(n) if nearly sorted | O(1) | Yes | Great for tiny/nearly-sorted data |
| Selection | O(n²) always | O(1) | No | Minimal swaps |
| MergeSort | O(n log n) | O(n) | Yes | Predictable |
| QuickSort | O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst | O(log n) | No | Fastest in practice |
| HeapSort | O(n log n) | O(1) | No | No worst-case blowup |

## Stability

A **stable** sort preserves the relative order of equal keys. Sort orders by date, then stably by customer → within each customer, orders stay date-sorted. LINQ's `OrderBy` **is stable**; `Array.Sort` **is not**.

## What .NET actually does

`Array.Sort`/`List.Sort` use **introsort**: QuickSort that switches to HeapSort when recursion gets too deep, and insertion sort for small partitions — engineering pragmatism over purity.

`OrderBy(...).ThenBy(...)` relies on stability — that's why `ThenBy` works at all.
items.practiceTask: Implement insertion sort. Verify it's O(n) on already-sorted input by counting comparisons on sorted vs shuffled arrays of 10,000 items.

quiz.question: You sort a list by Name with an unstable sort, then by Age. What's lost vs doing it with LINQ OrderBy(Age).ThenBy(Name)?
options: Equal-age people are no longer alphabetical
quiz.explanation: Unstable sorting scrambles equal keys; ThenBy semantics require stability to preserve the secondary order.

items.title: Sorting II: QuickSort and Partitioning
items.practiceTask: Implement QuickSelect(int[] a, int k) returning the k-th smallest element without fully sorting. Use it to find the median and 95th percentile of 100,000 random response times.

quiz.question: QuickSort degrades to O(n²) when:
options: Partitions are consistently maximally unbalanced (e.g., sorted input, first-element pivot)
The array doesn't fit in cache
quiz.explanation: If every partition splits n into 1 and n-1, you get n levels of O(n) work.

items.title: Sorting III: MergeSort and the Merge Pattern
items.practiceTask: Write a method that merges two already-sorted List<Order> by OrderDate into one sorted list in O(n+m), without calling Sort. This mirrors SQL Server's merge join.

quiz.question: SQL Server's Merge Join requires both inputs sorted because:
options: It advances two pointers through the inputs, which only works on sorted streams
quiz.explanation: Merge join is the two-pointer merge: compare current rows, advance the smaller side — sortedness makes that correct.

items.title: HashSet and Set Operations
items.bodyMarkdown:
# HashSet<T>

A hash table storing only keys: O(1) average `Add`, `Contains`, `Remove`. Duplicates are ignored — `Add` returns `false` if already present (a free "seen before?" check).

## Set algebra

```csharp
var a = new HashSet<int> { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
var b = new HashSet<int> { 3, 4, 5 };

a.IntersectWith(b);  // {3,4}      — in both
a.UnionWith(b);      // {1,2,3,4,5}
a.ExceptWith(b);     // {1,2}      — in a, not b
a.IsSubsetOf(b);
```

LINQ equivalents (`Intersect`, `Union`, `Except`) build sets internally — same idea, allocation per call.

## Classic uses

- **Dedup**: `new HashSet<string>(emails)`.
- **Diffing**: which IDs are in the request but not in the DB? `requestIds.Except(dbIds)` — this is the in-memory version of SQL's `EXCEPT` / `NOT EXISTS`.
- **First duplicate**: loop with `if (!seen.Add(x)) return x;`
- **O(1) membership** replacing `list.Contains` (which is O(n)) inside loops — same upgrade as Dictionary for lookups.

For sorted-order iteration use `SortedSet<T>` (red-black tree, O(log n) ops).
items.practiceTask: Given two lists of customer IDs (database vs import file), produce: new IDs (in file only), deleted IDs (in DB only), unchanged IDs — using HashSet operations. Compare with how you'd write it in SQL.

quiz.question: list.Contains(x) inside a loop over n items, where list has m items, costs overall:
quiz.explanation: Each Contains is a linear scan of m; doing it n times multiplies. A HashSet drops it to O(n + m).

items.title: Trees: Terminology and Recursive Thinking
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Trees

A hierarchy of nodes: one **root**, every other node has exactly one parent. **Leaf** = no children. **Height** = longest root-to-leaf path. A **binary tree** caps children at two (left/right).

You already work with trees daily:

- The DOM and Angular's component tree
- JSON payloads
- SQL Server execution plans (operators feeding parent operators)
- File systems, org charts, category hierarchies

## Trees are recursively defined

A tree is: a node + a list of subtrees. So tree algorithms are naturally recursive — handle the node, recurse on children:

```csharp
public class TreeNode
{
    public int Value;
    public TreeNode? Left, Right;
}

int Height(TreeNode? node) =>
    node == null ? 0 : 1 + Math.Max(Height(node.Left), Height(node.Right));

int Count(TreeNode? node) =>
    node == null ? 0 : 1 + Count(node.Left) + Count(node.Right);
```

Base case = null child. Progress = children are smaller trees. Both run in O(n) — every node visited once.

A **balanced** tree keeps height ≈ log n, which is what makes tree-based indexes fast; an unbalanced tree can degrade into a linked list (height n). That distinction drives the next lessons.
items.practiceTask: Model your product category hierarchy as a tree (or use a folder tree). Write recursive methods: CountLeaves, MaxDepth, and Find(name) returning the path from root.

quiz.question: A balanced binary tree with 1,000,000 nodes has height approximately:
quiz.explanation: Height ≈ log₂(n); log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20. That's why balanced trees give O(log n) operations.

items.title: Binary Search Trees: Ordered Data, O(log n) Ops
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Binary Search Trees

**BST invariant**: everything in the left subtree < node < everything in the right subtree. Search, insert, delete all follow one root-to-leaf path: **O(height)**.

```csharp
bool Contains(TreeNode? node, int value)
{
    if (node == null) return false;
    if (value == node.Value) return true;
    return value < node.Value
        ? Contains(node.Left, value)
        : Contains(node.Right, value);
}
```

## The balance problem

Insert 1,2,3,4,5 in order → every node goes right → a linked list with O(n) search. **Self-balancing** trees (AVL, red-black) rotate nodes on insert to keep height O(log n). .NET's `SortedDictionary<K,V>` and `SortedSet<T>` are red-black trees.

## BST vs Dictionary

| Need | Use |
|---|---|
| Fastest lookup, no ordering | `Dictionary` O(1) |
| Sorted iteration, range queries ("all keys between a and b"), min/max | `SortedDictionary`/`SortedSet` O(log n) |

## Useful property

**In-order traversal of a BST yields sorted order** — also the standard way to validate a BST: in-order values must be strictly increasing.

SQL Server's B-tree indexes are the same idea widened: each node holds hundreds of keys (one 8KB page), so the tree is shallow (3–4 levels for millions of rows).
items.practiceTask: Implement BST Insert and Contains. Insert 1..1000 in sorted order vs shuffled order; measure tree height both ways and observe degradation.

quiz.question: Inserting sorted data 1,2,3,...,n into a naive (non-balancing) BST results in:
options: A degenerate linked-list shape with O(n) operations
quiz.explanation: Every value is larger than the last, so each goes right — height becomes n.

items.title: Tree Traversals: Pre-, In-, Post-Order DFS
items.practiceTask: Build a small expression tree for (3 + 4) * 2 and evaluate it with post-order traversal. Then print the expression with in-order traversal (add parentheses).

quiz.question: To compute the total size of each folder in a directory tree (needing children's sizes first), which traversal fits?
quiz.explanation: Post-order visits children before the parent, so child sizes are available when the parent sums them.

items.title: BFS and Level-Order Traversal
items.practiceTask: Print your category/folder tree level by level, one line per level. Then answer: what's the minimum number of clicks (levels) to reach a given node? — that's BFS depth.

quiz.question: Why does BFS find shortest paths in unweighted graphs?
options: It explores all nodes at distance k before any node at distance k+1
quiz.explanation: The queue processes nodes in distance order, so the first arrival at any node used the fewest edges.

items.title: Heaps: PriorityQueue Internals and Top-K
items.practiceTask: Given 1M random numbers, find the 100 largest using a size-100 min-heap. Compare elapsed time against sorting the whole array and taking the last 100.

quiz.question: To find the K largest items in a huge stream, you keep a:
options: Min-heap of size K — evict the minimum when something bigger arrives
quiz.explanation: The heap's min is the 'weakest' of the current top-K; anything larger replaces it in O(log K).

items.title: Graphs: Modeling and Representations
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Graphs

Nodes (**vertices**) + connections (**edges**). Directed or undirected, weighted or not. Trees are just connected acyclic graphs.

You already own several graphs:

- Foreign keys between tables (dependency graph — what order to seed/delete?)
- Microservice call graphs
- npm/NuGet package dependencies
- Angular module imports
- "Users who follow users"

## Representations

```csharp
// Adjacency list — the default choice
var graph = new Dictionary<string, List<string>>
{
    ["Orders"] = ["Customers", "Products"],   // Orders depends on both
    ["Invoices"] = ["Orders"],
    ["Customers"] = [], ["Products"] = []
};
```

| Representation | Space | Edge lookup | Iterate neighbors | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adjacency list | O(V+E) | O(degree) | O(degree) | Sparse graphs (most real ones) |
| Adjacency matrix | O(V²) | O(1) | O(V) | Dense graphs, tiny V |

## Key vocabulary

- **Degree** — number of edges at a vertex.
- **Path / cycle** — sequence of edges; cycle returns to start.
- **Connected component** — set of mutually reachable nodes.
- **DAG** — directed acyclic graph; anything with a valid "build order" (migrations, task dependencies) is a DAG.
items.practiceTask: Write code that builds an adjacency list of your database's FK dependencies (table → tables it references). You'll reuse this graph in the next three lessons.

quiz.question: For a sparse graph with 10,000 vertices and 30,000 edges, an adjacency matrix would waste space because:
options: It stores O(V²) = 100M cells for only 30K edges
quiz.explanation: The matrix allocates a cell per vertex pair regardless of whether an edge exists; adjacency lists store only actual edges.

items.title: Graph DFS: Reachability, Cycles, Components
items.practiceTask: Using your FK dependency graph from the previous lesson, detect whether any circular table references exist, and list one cycle if found.

quiz.question: In directed-graph cycle detection, why isn't the plain visited set enough — why track nodes on the current path?
options: A node visited via a *different* completed path isn't a cycle; only revisiting the current path is
It is enough — the extra set is an optimization
quiz.explanation: Diamond shapes (A→B→D, A→C→D) revisit D without any cycle. Only a back edge to an ancestor on the active path proves one.

items.title: Graph BFS: Shortest Paths and Degrees of Separation
items.practiceTask: Build a small friendship graph (8-10 people) and write DegreesOfSeparation(a, b) using BFS with parent tracking; print the actual connection path.

quiz.question: Why must the visited check happen when enqueueing rather than when dequeuing (for efficiency)?
options: It's required for correctness of the path
Otherwise the same node can be enqueued many times, bloating the queue
It changes the complexity class to O(1)
quiz.explanation: Marking on enqueue guarantees each node enters the queue once; marking on dequeue allows duplicates in the queue (still correct, but wasteful).

items.title: Topological Sort: Dependency Ordering
items.practiceTask: Feed your FK dependency graph into Kahn's algorithm to produce a safe seeding order (referenced tables first), then reverse it for a safe deletion order. Verify against your real database.

quiz.question: Kahn's algorithm finishes with fewer nodes in the output than the graph contains. This means:
options: The graph contains a cycle — remaining nodes never reached in-degree 0
quiz.explanation: Nodes in a cycle keep positive in-degree forever, so they're never enqueued — a built-in cycle test.

items.title: Dijkstra: Shortest Paths with Weights
items.practiceTask: Model 6-8 cities with weighted roads and implement Dijkstra with path reconstruction (track parent on each relaxation). Verify the cheapest path by hand.

quiz.question: Dijkstra fails with negative edge weights because:
options: Priority queues can't hold negative numbers
Once a node is settled as 'closest', a cheaper path through a negative edge may appear later — violating the greedy assumption
It only fails with negative cycles, not negative edges
quiz.explanation: The greedy proof assumes settled distances are final; negative edges can undercut them afterward.

items.title: Prefix Sums: Precompute Once, Answer Instantly
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Prefix Sums

Precompute running totals so any **range sum** becomes O(1):

```csharp
// prefix[i] = sum of arr[0..i-1]
var prefix = new long[arr.Length + 1];
for (int i = 0; i < arr.Length; i++)
    prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + arr[i];

long RangeSum(int from, int to) => prefix[to + 1] - prefix[from];  // inclusive
```

Build O(n) once → each query O(1). Without it, q queries cost O(n×q).

## Variants

- **2D prefix sums** — O(1) sum of any rectangle (image processing, heatmaps).
- **Difference array** — the inverse: apply "add x to range [a,b]" in O(1) per update, reconstruct with one prefix pass. Perfect for "count overlapping bookings per day".
- **Prefix + HashMap** — count subarrays summing to k: store counts of prefix values seen; `prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k` means look up `prefix[j] - k`.

## Where it shows up for you

Running totals in SQL (`SUM(...) OVER (ORDER BY date)`) are prefix sums. Dashboard "revenue between any two dates" from a precomputed daily cumulative table = this exact trick, and why materialized running totals make range reports instant.
items.practiceTask: Given a year of daily sales, build a prefix array and answer 5 arbitrary date-range sum queries in O(1) each. Then write the equivalent SQL window function and compare results.

quiz.question: With prefix sums built, the sum of elements from index 3 to 7 inclusive is:
quiz.explanation: prefix[8] covers indices 0..7; subtracting prefix[3] (indices 0..2) leaves exactly 3..7.

items.title: Backtracking: Systematic Trial and Error
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Backtracking

Explore choices depth-first; when a partial solution can't lead anywhere valid, **undo the last choice and try the next**. It's DFS over the tree of partial solutions.

## Template

```csharp
void Backtrack(List<int> current, /* remaining state */)
{
    if (IsComplete(current)) { results.Add(new(current)); return; }
    foreach (var choice in ValidChoices(current))
    {
        current.Add(choice);        // choose
        Backtrack(current);         // explore
        current.RemoveAt(current.Count - 1);  // un-choose ← the "backtrack"
    }
}
```

The undo step is what distinguishes it from plain recursion — one shared `current` list mutated and restored, instead of copying state every call.

## Classic problems

- All permutations / combinations / subsets
- N-Queens, Sudoku solvers
- Word search in a grid (mark cell used, recurse, unmark)
- Constraint problems: scheduling meetings into rooms, assigning shifts

## Pruning is the whole game

Worst case is exponential — the wins come from rejecting doomed branches **early** (e.g., N-Queens: check column/diagonal conflicts before placing, not after completing the board). Good pruning turns "impossible" into "milliseconds".
items.practiceTask: Generate all subsets of {1..n} with the template, then solve: all combinations of k numbers from 1..n that sum to target — add pruning (stop when sum exceeds target) and count how many branches pruning eliminates.

quiz.question: The step that makes it 'backtracking' rather than plain DFS is:
options: Undoing the choice after the recursive call returns
quiz.explanation: State is mutated before recursing and restored after — allowing the shared state to be reused across branches.

items.title: Dynamic Programming I: Memoization
items.practiceTask: Solve coin change (minimum coins for amount 87 with coins {1,5,10,25}) with naive recursion first — note the slowness for large amounts — then add memoization and compare call counts.

quiz.question: Memoization improves naive Fibonacci from O(2ⁿ) to O(n) because:
options: Each distinct subproblem (n states) is computed once; repeats become O(1) lookups
quiz.explanation: There are only n distinct inputs; caching bounds total work at one computation per input.

items.title: Dynamic Programming II: Tabulation and Classic Problems
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Bottom-Up DP (Tabulation)

Instead of recursing down, fill a table from base cases **up**. No recursion, no stack limits, often optimizable to O(1) space.

```csharp
// Fibonacci with two variables — O(n) time, O(1) space
long a = 0, b = 1;
for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) (a, b) = (b, a + b);
```

## Canonical problems worth knowing

**0/1 Knapsack** — items with weight/value, capacity W:
`dp[i][w]` = best value using first i items within weight w:
`dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w - weight[i]] + value[i])`

**Longest Common Subsequence** (diff tools, DNA alignment):
`dp[i][j] = chars match ? dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 : max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])`

**Edit distance** (spell-check, fuzzy matching) — same table shape, three operations.

**House robber** (no two adjacent): `dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + val[i])`

## Memoization vs tabulation

| | Top-down (memo) | Bottom-up (table) |
|---|---|---|
| Writing it | Easier — natural recursion | Requires knowing fill order |
| Computes | Only needed states | All states |
| Stack overflow risk | Yes, deep inputs | No |
| Space optimization | Hard | Often easy (keep last row) |

Interviews accept either; production code favors tabulation for hot paths.
items.practiceTask: Implement LCS length for two strings bottom-up. Then extend it to output the actual subsequence by walking the table backwards — this is the core of every diff tool.

quiz.question: In 0/1 knapsack, dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-wᵢ] + vᵢ). The two arms represent:
options: Taking item i twice vs once
Skipping item i vs taking it (paying its weight, gaining its value)
quiz.explanation: Every item admits exactly two futures — excluded or included — and the recurrence takes the better.

items.title: Union-Find: Dynamic Connectivity
items.practiceTask: Given a list of (email, accountId) pairs, union accounts sharing an email and print the resulting customer groups. This is real-world identity resolution in ~30 lines.

quiz.question: Path compression speeds up Union-Find by:
options: Re-pointing every node visited during Find directly at the root, flattening the tree
Using a hash table instead of an array
quiz.explanation: After one Find, all nodes on the path hang off the root — future Finds on them are O(1).

items.title: Choosing the Right Data Structure: A Decision Framework
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Decision Framework

Stop asking "what's clever"; ask **"what operations dominate?"**

| Dominant need | Reach for | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Lookup by key | `Dictionary<K,V>` | O(1) |
| Membership test / dedup | `HashSet<T>` | O(1) |
| Ordered iteration + range queries | `SortedDictionary` / `SortedSet` | O(log n) |
| Index access, iteration, append | `List<T>` / arrays | O(1) |
| FIFO processing | `Queue<T>` | O(1) |
| LIFO / undo / nesting | `Stack<T>` | O(1) |
| Repeated min/max extraction, top-K | `PriorityQueue<T,P>` | O(log n) |
| Both-ends insertion | `LinkedList<T>` | O(1) at known nodes |
| Concurrent access | `ConcurrentDictionary`, `Channel<T>` | varies |

## Red flags in code review (your own included)

- `list.Contains(...)` or `FirstOrDefault(...)` **inside a loop** → Dictionary/HashSet built once.
- Repeated `OrderBy().First()` → PriorityQueue or a single pass Min.
- `string +=` in a loop → StringBuilder.
- `Insert(0, item)` on a List in a loop → Queue or reverse-then-append: each front-insert is O(n).
- Deeply nested loops matching two collections → join via Dictionary (this is a hash join — exactly what SQL Server chooses too).

## The 90% rule

In typical ASP.NET Core services, `List<T>` + `Dictionary<K,V>` + `HashSet<T>` cover ~90% of needs. Mastery isn't using exotic structures — it's instantly recognizing the 10% where a heap, sorted tree, or graph turns O(n²) into O(n log n).
items.practiceTask: Audit one service class in your current project. For every collection, write down the dominant operations and verify the chosen structure matches this table. Fix at least one mismatch.

quiz.question: Code calls orders.FirstOrDefault(o => o.Id == id) inside a foreach over 10,000 customers with 50,000 orders. Best fix?
options: Build a Dictionary<int,Order> once before the loop — turning O(n×m) into O(n+m)
quiz.explanation: One O(m) dictionary build converts every in-loop lookup to O(1) — the single most impactful everyday optimization.
